title: QUIC CRT trim and Native AOT compatibility architecture
purpose: Describe how the shipped Incursa.Quic package stays trimmable and Native AOT compatible when downstream consumers reference it through PackageReference on the supported net10.0 target.
risks: If trim and AOT analyzers are only considered for direct project references, package-only regressions can slip through unnoticed.
If reflection or runtime code generation is introduced in a helper path, the library can remain buildable while becoming unsafe for Native AOT consumers.
If the package boundary is judged only by the source tree and not by a PackageReference consumer, downstream publish-time warnings can remain hidden until release.
design_summary: The package compatibility slice treats the shipped NuGet package as the unit of compatibility, not just the repo project. The net10.0 build enables the trim and AOT analyzers for the library, the public API remains the only supported consumer surface, and the implementation avoids reflection, runtime code generation, and other patterns that would force the trimmer or AOT compiler to preserve unsupported code. Verification exercises a downstream PackageReference consumer so trimming and Native AOT are validated at the same package boundary that downstream apps will use.
